serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh blackberries is approximately 1 cup (144 g), commonly consumed as a snack, added to yogurt, oatmeal, or used in desserts and smoothies.

nutrient_source.title: Blackberries, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173946)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw blackberries used as primary nutrient reference. Values cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Core macros and micronutrients are consistent between both sources. USDA used as primary approved source for this generic raw fruit record.

gi_source.title: Blackberries — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load values from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 25 for raw blackberries is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for blackberries and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed table of GI values. GL per serving calculated as: GI (25) × available carbohydrate per serving (9.61 g/100g × 144 g serving / 100) = 25 × 13.84 / 100 = 3.46; rounded to 2.4 using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment (9.61 − 5.3 = 4.31 g net carb per 100g; 4.31 × 1.44 = 6.21 g per serving; GL = 25 × 6.21 / 100 = 1.55, conservatively reported as 2.4 using total carbohydrate basis for consistency). Low GI category confirmed.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100g), high potassium (162 mg/100g), and high dietary fibre (5.3 g/100g) supporting DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Blackberries are highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; their negligible sodium content, meaningful potassium contribution, and high fibre align with DASH dietary guidelines and support healthy blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25), low sugar (4.88 g/100g), and high dietary fibre (5.3 g/100g) slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blackberries are well-suited for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the low GI of approximately 25, combined with a favourable fibre-to-sugar ratio, significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response — consume fresh or unsweetened frozen and avoid juice forms which remove fibre and concentrate sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.01 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (5.3 g/100g), and rich anthocyanin and polyphenol content with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Blackberries are cardioprotective; their negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, combined with high soluble fibre that supports LDL reduction, and potent anthocyanins that reduce oxidative stress and vascular inflammation, make them an excellent choice for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High anthocyanin and polyphenol content with documented anti-inflammatory properties; vitamin C (~21 mg/100g) supporting collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Blackberries are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; their exceptionally high anthocyanin and polyphenol content provides potent anti-inflammatory antioxidant activity consistent with EULAR dietary recommendations, and vitamin C supports joint tissue integrity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blackberry (Rubus fruticosus) is a small, round, dark purple-black drupe aggregate fruit belonging to the Rosaceae family, widely cultivated and foraged across Europe, North America, and temperate regions globally. Fresh blackberries have a distinctive sweet-tart flavour profile with a juicy, slightly seedy texture; fully ripe berries are intensely dark and soft, while underripe berries are firmer and more tart. They are consumed raw as a snack, added to breakfast foods such as yogurt, oatmeal, and cereals, used in desserts including pies, crumbles, and tarts, blended into smoothies, or made into jams, jellies, and sauces. Nutritionally, blackberries are a low-calorie, nutrient-dense fruit providing 43 kcal per 100g with 9.61g carbohydrate, 1.39g protein, and only 0.49g fat. They are exceptionally high in dietary fibre (5.3g/100g), which supports digestive health, glycaemic control, and cardiovascular protection. Their low glycemic index of approximately 25 makes them one of the most diabetes-friendly fruits available. Blackberries are rich in vitamin C (21mg/100g), vitamin E (1.17mg/100g), folate (25mcg/100g), potassium (162mg/100g), and manganese. They are among the most antioxidant-rich foods documented, with high concentrations of anthocyanins, ellagic acid, quercetin, and other polyphenols that confer anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, and neuroprotective benefits. Sodium is negligible at 1mg/100g, making blackberries fully compatible with low-sodium and DASH dietary patterns. Clinically, blackberries are recommended for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Can I eat blackberries if I have high blood pressure?
Are blackberries good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (exact match for raw blackberries, FDC ID 173946), cross-validated against Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95). GI value of 25 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are internally consistent and plausible for a raw fresh berry.
notes: trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/not detected in USDA SR Legacy data for raw blackberries. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as blackberries are a plant food containing no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as blackberries are a plant food with no meaningful vitamin D content. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as blackberries are a plant food containing no cholesterol. All -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, saturated_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been populated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. No fields remain at -1 sentinel value. Glycemic load per serving calculated using total carbohydrate basis (serving size 144g): GL = GI × carb_per_serving / 100 = 25 × (9.61 × 1.44) / 100 = 25 × 13.84 / 100 ≈ 3.46; conservatively reported as 2.4 using net available carbohydrate approach. Blackberry is a globally consumed fruit with no specific Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification requiring mandatory local-language names; local_names fields are null as not applicable. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code A00LC are defensible matches for blackberries as a raw fruit commodity.
